solution: This difference quotient is the derivative of \( f(x) = x^{[[n]]} \) at \( x = [[a]] \). Since \( f'(x) = [[n]]x^{[[n]]-1} \), the limit is \( [[n]]\cdot[[a]]^{[[n]]-1} = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This evaluates \( f([[a]]) \), the function value, instead of its derivative.
This keeps the full power \( a^{n} \) instead of reducing it by one.
This forgets to multiply by the exponent \( n \).
This lowers the exponent used as the coefficient by one.
This is off by one from the correct value.
This is off by one from the correct value.

solution: This difference quotient is the derivative of \( f(x) = e^{x} \) at \( x = [[a]] \). Since \( f'(x) = e^{x} \), the limit is \( f'([[a]]) = e^{[[a]]} = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This multiplies the correct value by \( a \), confusing \( f'(x)=e^{x} \) with the derivative of \( a e^{x} \).
This multiplies \( a \) by \( e \) instead of exponentiating.
This has the wrong sign in the exponent.
This shifts the exponent by one instead of leaving it unchanged.
This evaluates the exponent alone rather than \( e \) raised to it.
This drops the exponent \( a \) entirely.

solution: This difference quotient is the derivative of \( f(x) = \dfrac{1}{x} \) at \( x = [[a]] \). Since \( f'(x) = -\dfrac{1}{x^{2}} \), the limit is \( f'([[a]]) = -\dfrac{1}{[[a]]^{2}} = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This drops the minus sign from \( f'(x) = -1/x^{2} \).
This uses \( f(x)=1/x \) itself rather than its derivative.
This flips both the sign and the missing square.
This confuses the derivative with \( -a \) instead of \( -1/a^{2} \).
This is off by one from the correct value.
This is off by one from the correct value.

solution: This difference quotient is the derivative of \( f(x) = \ln(x) \) at \( x = [[a]] \). Since \( f'(x) = \dfrac{1}{x} \), the limit is \( f'([[a]]) = \dfrac{1}{[[a]]} = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This confuses the derivative \( 1/a \) with \( a \) itself.
This has the wrong sign.
This squares the denominator, confusing \( \ln \) with \( 1/x \)'s derivative rule.
This returns \( \ln(a) \), the function value, instead of the derivative.
This is off by one from the correct value.
This is off by one from the correct value.

solution: This difference quotient is the derivative of \( f(x) = \sin(x) \) at \( x = \theta_0 \). Since \( f'(x) = \cos(x) \), the limit is \( \cos(\theta_0) = \cos\!\left(\dfrac{[[k]]\pi}{2}\right) = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This returns \( \sin(\theta_0) \), the function value, instead of its derivative \( \cos(\theta_0) \).
This confuses the answer with the raw index \( k \) shifted by 10.
This confuses the answer with the raw index \( k \) shifted by -10.
This confuses the answer with a linear function of the index \( k \) instead of \( \cos(k\pi/2) \).
This is off by 5 from the correct value.
This is off by 5 from the correct value.
